author: @kreynolds1123
text:
I see one thing that concerns me. Air flows only half the time. This could be fixed with two bellows  one contracting as the other is expanding. That way you get a more continous air flow. 

The valve chest and bellows could be combined into one unit, a bellows with a valve at each opening.

author: @rogerbarton497
text: If you make the inlet valve smaller than the outlet valve the air will cool as it goes from a higher pressure to a lower one, but it will not be heated again by compression because there will less resistance on the bigger exhaust valve.  The downside is that you may need a more powerful motor, but you may be able to do without the heat exchanger.  I like the idea of the bellows wheezing away in the corner of my living room.

author: @johnmorrison6814
text:
We have ducted airconditioning in our house.  
What I've always thought is :
Why don't they add a valve (the standard aircon ones they use already) on the incoming side to drag air from outside the house if it's closer to the set temperature?
Potential there could be more inputs dragging hot/cold air from different parts reducing the need to run the compressor and saving power.

author: @KerrFamilyLaw
text: There is now in some places and there will be soon in other places a Covid-19 based oxygen shortage. If it were possible to ameliorate this shortage on a very local basis, this could save countless lives. Seems to me that your bellows can be hand-made in the smallest most rural villages, and dc motors are available everywhere, as are batteries and and if no batteries or generator can be found, even a human-powered bicycle could run a bellows to blow cool oxygen-rich air across the face of a person having low oxygen saturation and no medical care. All we need is your brain power to teach the world how to produce oxygen-rich air in the middle of nowhere, with items that can be found in every home, or in every village.....

author: @NorthernKitty
text: This video is one of the best examples as to what I enjoy about your content.  First, your modular approach of breaking everything down into individual components and building blocks.  Next, you discuss a wide range of options in addition to showing us those options both in construction and in practice.  Finally, you prompt us to think about "what do we have available?"  What your channel does differently from most is to equip us with a wide variety of tools and ideas that will help us arrive at the best solution for ourselves.  I find this preferable versus handing us a "canned" solution as most do on YouTube.  However, I suspect that we, your subscribers, are part of the minority in that regard.  Most people are in a hurry to find a "quick fix", "best performance" or "most novel" solution.  Or they're just looking to be entertained.  Which makes you all the more invaluable to us - your content is a breath of fresh air to "makers" looking for inspiration and ideas.  If I were to make a "zen" analogy, you are the journey where most content creators on YouTube are trying to be the destination.

author: @jamest.5001
text: I had a crazy idea when I discovered the diesel heaters, having something like that that can heat a vehicle, or camper, and insted of requiring 12v or 24v and fuel to work, I was thinking it would be awesome if it generated power! While thermal electric are not efficient, but a small engine gives off alot of heat, and can generate power, if it could do just 300-500 watts electrically it would be  very welcome, with enough to keep a room warm mid winter, even better!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I lIke CHP - I thinks it's a good answer

replies.author: @shaywave
replies.text:
Yes, this is called "cogeneration" and because you are using the waste heat from an engine your overall system efficiency can be very good.  In fact, a 7000w gen-set produces enough waste heat to keep a small house warm through the winter and supply all the electrical needs as well.  

- Chaos

replies.author: @jamest.5001
replies.text: @shaywave I was thinking smaller, in the 1-2kw range, to around 5kw max. My off grid house is basically setup like a rv, I'm looking for a heat source that can also help charge the batteries! Winter isn't that bad here, 3months at best needing any heat,   a smslle water-cooled engine would be awesome ..

replies.author: @shaywave
replies.text:
@jamest.5001  For an RV sized house 1kw should work, 2kw will definately work.  A water cooled gen set is the best for this because the hot engine water can be stored to heat your house without having to run the engine.  However, a water cooled unit might be unavailable in such a small size.  You might have to get creative there.  

It is too bad commercially available co-gen units aren't available, ready to go, for the off grid user.

All the best,
- Chaos.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
All the conversion losses is why I use 12/24v led lighting, I have a 24volt main system, with inverter, and a smaller 12v battery bank, (200+ah lithium,) for the lighting, no need to go to HV AC then to what ever the led bulbs require, I still have those if needed, where I live it is about like Florida, mid summer, minus the cool ocean breeze, it's hot and humid here! For about about month anyway, then it will be about 80° days, it's 90's now, it doesn't get cold until the end of November! And doesn't snow until January February, 'if' it snows'!!  Its not a sure thing in this area, 
But off grid is nice if you have the time, and experience,  and multiple power sources! For back up!! And a wood stove!

When I first started generating my own power, from the sun, it was a great feeling!  And cutting losses is a plus anytime!

author: @justinw1765
text:
I highly suggest that folks go to Tech Ingredients channel and check out his two videos on DIY air conditioning using a combo of desiccant, evaporation, fans, heat, radiators, and pumps.  


  I watched both and after awhile, I came up with a much more simpler and less energy intensive system.  You keep the two PVC towers with calcium chloride as the desiccant plus shower heads plus fans and pumps, but get rid of the more complicated Solar heater and then radiator cooler.  


     Instead, have a fish tank painted black with carbon felt floating on top.  This is the desiccant concentrater and heater.  Pump this water to coiled copper tubing buried under ground for a few feet (to cool off the water), pump that to the 2nd tower to evaporate and cool some more and then to the 1st tower to further that process and which pumps the cooler, drier air back into the house and which also receives the inside air. 


      Also different than TI's system, I would also add Xylitol and/or Erythritol  to the water along with the calcium chloride, provided they don't react with each other (I don't know about this yet).  

  My spouse vetoed this project since she didn't want PVC towers outside of our windows.  Never mind that we live on a private road with a lot of trees and coverage...  But she is like 90%  aesthetic oriented and 10% practical/pragmatic, and I'm the exact opposite.  But I still plan to build a mini, portable version for our pickup truck and top mini "camper" for late Spring to early Fall car camping trips. I live in the humid and hot southeast of the US.  It's way too hot and humid for me much of the time otherwise (which is why I backpack from mid'ish fall to early spring).

replies.author: @boots7859
replies.text: Justin, yeah TI really had a great idea going. I'm disappointed in how he seemed to write it off though.  I know it was do to efficiency, however really interested in how you seem to have overcome some of the issues. Any chance you could post a video or add it to Instructables?

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@boots7859  Hi, thank you for the interest and yes, I agree with your summation of TI. Eventually I would like to post a video on it.  I have too many irons in the fire at the moment though.  I'm still in the process of building said camper top for the pickup truck (XPS foam+fiberglass+Titebond 3 primarily).  

  Also am designing a super solar heater system, which I finally received a necessary component for, after receiving one which was falsely advertised (Bnunwish so called "vacuum insulated" borosilicate coffee pot, which was definitely NOT vacuum insulated).  

  That's my next project and first video to do. 

  The problem is is that I lack the instrumentation that TI has and unlike him, am rather materially poor.  

  One of my main projects, that hopefully will net some money, is producing carbonized and graphitized cellulose nanocrystals for battery, super capacitor, composites, etc.  If I was smart, I would just work on that to the exclusion of most everything else for awhile, so I could make and save up some money.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: P.S., the solar heater system is for the initial carbonization of the CNC.  But the processing of same is a somewhat laborious and time consuming process.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Just like how increasing cylinders in an engine smooths out the power generation to a crank shaft, multiple bellows, especially if you intentionally time them off of a common crank shaft, each with its own valve, THEN through a manifold, and you would get a smoother, less intermittent flow of air.

That is the only real benefit to using more bellows as opposed to making one larger bellows, but  depending on how one is rigging the system up, it might also provide better space management, say, for instance, you needed to put it on a wall, or on a ceiling so as to not take up too much floor space some place where space might be limited.

Well, I do suppose, just possibly, one could immerse the bellows in a cooling chamber [far less efficient than using a radiator, of course] with something like cool running water, but it could be a tiring problem to seal it so it is not messy, so yeah, a sealed radiator would indeed be more convenient.

Also, a radiator need not be a pre-made one, if you have a hard time getting one, you could make a radiator with multiple thin sheets of any material which efficiently conducts heat.

THIS is the TRULY FANTASTIC point...where you could use your own home made GRAPHENE [assuming you made it well enough to ensure it exhibits at least somewhat good thermal conductivity...] and form large flat [or corrugated, if you fancy and care to spend the extra time or feel you need much more surface area] hollow panels [see link to illustration] where cold air, water, or refrigerant [which, interestingly, can ALSO be 'cold air, simply after it has been stored as you would inside of a 'liquid air battery', since in the process of liquefying it, you tremendously increase its potential to RE-absorb heat!] can flow through a manifold to multiple panels [of dimensions you choose on your own] while the air being cooled is being drawn on the 'outside' of them, but from inside some sort of box, so as to ensure the air has the best possible chance of getting cooled before being taken into the pump, or exhausting from the radiator into a room, FROM the pump. [the radiator can be either before, or behind the valve and pump assembly, just depends on which way most efficiently suits your needs.

https://www.graphene-info.com/graphene-thermal

I am not sure, but you MAY be able to ALSO use graphene to make pressure tanks, but PLEASE DO NOT hold me to that, I know too little about its mechanical properties to try to guess if it is remotely as strong as carbon fiber... It merely SEEMS like it might.

I suppose, in a pinch, one could quickly make a stack of flat heat exchange tubes out of common steel stove pipe, especially if they have some way to carefully draw-flatten it out, like a strong block of wood, plywood or such which can have some stiff pipe or tube, or rod or dowel glued to each end, which is formed part of the way in a taper [so you can get it into the stove pipe or other thin walled hollow tubing] then going to a somewhat parallel end, just to make sure the whole length of your flattened tube is all the same dimension and easy to mount without a lot of fussing to make it fit and without having to cut custom openings in the box walls to get it in.

One could likely seal something like this with silicone sealant, epoxy, an extremely expensive amount of hot glue, or, if it is all metal, solder or brazing rod [hearkening back to Robert's two very recent videos about joining copper to aluminum and aluminum to aluminum https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AJ4tGAGB968 and https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tPSet8HaMJw ]

Between aluminum, copper and graphene, you could really achieve some good thermal efficiency, if A you can afford it, B, you can find it cheaply used, or C, you can 'repurpose' some item which contains enough to produce the final size of product you need.

I fully think this is a super wonderful line of thinking and for me, honestly, it refreshes my mind to see this and express ideas, it really is something people should be watching!

Thank you so very much again Robert for taking this time to help all of us expand our minds and explore the universe of infinite possibilities!

By the way, I ALSO figured out a SUPER EFFICIENT and yet SUPER SIMPLE way to use the rubber band heat pump you described in this video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MaY0bzlq_GI

Basically, all one really needs, is 2 separate air flows, going past one rubber band, spinning it tight then loose over and over [as long as the rubber band lasts anyhow, and that might be a long long time, depending on a lot of unknown conditions] so one driving force [be it a motor, a wind turbine, a water wheel, or a living being turning a crank, matters not] can both wind the rubber band, operate the bellows, and any valves which one may decide need to be operated by a method other than a flapper valve, but in all, it would be stupidly simple, could be made mostly out of recycled materials, and would have nothing toxic, flammable, or hard to locate so as you like to say "ANYBODY could build it!"

The simple trick is, on the same motor that runs your crank [to move the bellows] you can attach from the other end [assuming it has 2 outputs, otherwise you need to rig a shaft with a pull or gear or something on the single output for each application, OR simply connect the rubber band driver to the rotating axle shaft of the bellows driver by installing another wheel or crank lever outside of the crank pin/throw, so that it can continue to direct a portion of the force back into rotary motion on the far side [it just occurred to me, you could make the bellows by re-purposing a beat up old 1 or 2 cylinder small engine like you might find at a scrap yard or from a cheap old mower from a yard sale, and if the flow were too slow, just get an air compressor adapter kit and make it into a normal air compressor, would be really cheap, and would work pretty well, although you may want to try to put some Teflon tape around the rings to increase piston seal since such slow speeds would otherwise cause a lot of leak-out...] but the trick is, make a 'screw-release' [or you might say a 'screw driven catch release' ] which spins an exact number of times before bumping a release on the clutch holding the rubber band, allowing it to fully unwind [causing it to reabsorb heat, if you remember] while at the same time, swapping a couple of valves, to ensure the air is flowing the proper direction.

If you can get something like one of these https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MnPCIkOeIVU or make one, which is designed to be timed to ensure you get the desired turns on your rubber band, you can have it geared down maybe, so ensure it travels far enough to allow the rubber band time enough to wind up, and also pushes the proper lever to rock the valves from intake to exhaust. [I made a drawing of it, and should scan it, if enough people tell me they are interested in seeing it.]

Basically the same motor moves the bellows, spins the rubber band, and possibly drives some of the valves, but a clutch releases the rubber band, and this removes the need for anything to be in control of its unwinding. If you feel it should unwind slowly, just put a viscous brake on it [like some washers (or other disks) with grease between them that create viscous drag to resist free-spin] and with a sprag clutch, you can make this feature [the drag brake]  not interfere with the winding [which if it were there (the drag brake) it would reduce operational efficiency, so it it is not there, it will waste less energy, and to make it 'seem' not there on the winding cycle, and yet still be there when one is winding it up, simply add a drag brake. I just remembered! Old dial type telephones had a centrifugal drag clutch in them, it was like a tiny clutch one might use on a small RC engine, but they are pretty weak, so this type project is probably more their strength.]

author: @michaeltucker8645
text: I have a portable unit in  my 12Lx15Wx10T shop I had to either dig out 25 30 inches in 1 corner or set it on 4x4 foundation blocks . So I decided the blocks I knew about those cons about the portable.  So I took my LG unit apart for the most part. I built a vented metal box in the crawl space. That I put the business end of the portable unit inside. Extended the wiring for thermostat and control panel and mounted it inside on the wall .  I put plastic on both sides of my frame work so I just picked 2 studs and cut the plastic on 1 side and added another layer of 6 mil plastic and silicone it air tight.  Cutting a vent at the top of that panel for the output of the unit.  I had to build a box for the input side and did so from underneath putting the input vent in the dead center of the floor. I made my own vent cover out of steel and recessed it into the flooring so my chair would still roll over it and not catch.  The distance was about the same for each vent from the unit.  It didn't effect it working at all . My unit had to have a dehumidifier bc of all the electronics that are stored there. You could do the exact same with a unit like this with 2 possible input vents take from air under the shop to cool down the shop til it gets to wanted temp then switch inputs to just circulate the air in the shop. I've been researching 12v solutions for ac and heat the unit and my power tool batteries are the only things left that dont run off my solar batteries

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: so basically you took a combined unit and split it?

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: Exactly

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: I extended the control panel especially the temperature sensor like hvac temperature sensor reads the temp at the height its mount 30° at 5 foot high might end up being 32 or 33 at 7 and 8 feet. Then just made ducts to control direction of air flow.  I'm 6 foot my shop 9.5 foot high walls so I mounted the control panel comfortably within reach above my head.  Isolating the heat produced completely outside.. used all left over material and bought the 14,000 btu  unit at a yard sale for 40 bucks .. I have another unit I got today 11,000 btu  I got for 40 as well. I'm gonna tare it down and see if I can convert it to 12v without using an inverters

author: @rotorspinny5516
text: Surely a fan would be far far far more efficient than a bellows system

replies.author: @colouroboros9993
replies.text: sure, but does it look as cool a s a bellows system and sound like breathing?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't know mate - it is dangerous to assume that - and what do you mean by efficient ? - I haven't measured it yet but I was thinking of using volume of air sjifthed against watt hours consumed - seemed like a fair measure to me - and then we could look at cost of the two ideas and maybe ease of construction - anyway - right now I have no idea and I am really just exploring

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @Rotor Spinny, It's hard to say.  There was a build on DIY Perks of a bellows system ("Building the World's Weirdest Fan") where he compared the volume of air that his bellows could move versus 4 x 140mm fans, and they performed at about the same rate, both with and without resistance.  In his design, he used a series of water pumps to move his bellows that consumed about 10 watts.  While he didn't specify it, I know from experience that fans the size he used likely consume around 2-4 watts each (8-16 watts in total), depending on the model.  His conclusion was that they were comparable in performance, except by size.  However, his method of driving the bellows was not necessarily the most efficient method.  He sacrificed a direct application of power to achieve both a quieter and more aesthetically pleasing design.  (Edit)  I'll add, without space being a constraint, I'd take the bellows hands down for quietness, aesthetics and less turbulent airflow.  And I honestly think you could achieve greater efficiency as regards watts per cfm of airflow.

replies.author: @rotorspinny5516
replies.text: Cats Pajamas interesting, I only commented because I’m in the Airconditioning industry where efficient quiet air flow is everything, and fans and blowers are all that’s used. So I assumed any method with articulated or mechanical moving parts would be less efficient and create noise 👌

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @rotorspinny5516 I absolutely could be wrong, these are just two anecdotal builds with very limited data and a lot of estimation happening.  The results merely warrant further investigation.  But especially after watching the DIY Perks build, I'm suspecting that size is the key factor as to why we've preferred fans and blowers to move air.  His estimate was that his bellows occupied twenty times the space, and I think he was being kind.  You wouldn't be able to hang an air conditioner built on bellows but of equal flow with that of a fan driven system out a standard window sill.  And square footage costs inside a building are at a premium.  Is it worth sacrificing $7,500 worth of floor space to save $100 per year on the ventilation method?  And think of all the extra material, shipping and installation costs in something that size if you were to purchase it "new" from the manufacturer.  So I think the size just hasn't been practical for most applications.  But when it comes to DIY...  well, there's a lot of ingenious ways to recover previously unused materials/space or to overcome obstacles and end up with dual-purpose solutions and/or functional art. 😄

replies.author: @shaywave
replies.text:
OMG, That is a good one. I still laughing! Too funny!

Actually, before electricity windows were the only way to cool your house.   The double sash window was invented for just this.  The idea is to bring in cool air and let out the hot.  By lowering the upper sash and raising the lower sash the hot air near the ceiling is let out and is replaced by cool air from the lower sash.  It is very simple and is 100% self powered.

author: @alejandrayalanbowman367
text: We like and use the system that the Moors used.  Have coolish air coming in at the lowest level of the building, ideally passing through fountains to cool, clean and moisten the air.  The air then passes up through the building and exits through openings at the top of the building.  It is all automatic using no power whatsoever.  Our house is on five levels from the patio at tha bottom right up though the building to the attic.

replies.author: @douglasfuqua7082
replies.text: interior cooling tower... simple...genius.  American Indians in southwest (Hot) USA used the same principle in cave dwellings, teepees, etc.  Thank you for your comment.  Love it.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: No power, but it does consume water. You're talking about evaporation cooling. It's very effective in the right climate, but that's the only good thing to be said about it - it doesn't work unless you can evaporate the water, which needs nice dry air. If you evaporate into moist air you not only get worse cooling, you get saturated air, which means sweat can't evaporate and you end up hot and dripping anyway.
